text:
## Overall Arc

O'Rourke's Complete Chord Melody is organized as a top-down, recipe-first method that begins with a working arrangement procedure on page one and only later supplies the theory and vocabulary needed to execute it. Chapter 1 (Module 1) opens with the four-step chord-melody method - pick a suitable tune, transpose it into a workable key, learn the melody on the top 2-3 strings, then add chord voicings below the melody notes - which functions as the book's operating system; every later chapter feeds inputs into one of those four steps. Chapter 2 (Module 2) builds the chord vocabulary the method indexes against, organized around the Three Harmonic Families taxonomy (Major / Minor / Dominant) and the chord collection construct that makes the dictionary lookup-able by melody note. Chapters 3-4 (Module 3) execute an end-to-end arrangement workflow, splitting it into a front half of melody-fingering, comping, and harmonic analysis (Chapter 3) and a back half that formalizes the load-bearing 'painting by numbers' rule and its remediation strategies (Chapter 4). Chapter 5 (Module 4) transitions from trio chord-melody to solo guitar by adding bass responsibility, shell voicings, and four core devices (arpeggiation, octaves, diads, walking bass). Chapter 6 closes Module 4 with annotated example arrangements as study material. Chapter 7 (Module 5) is the advanced reharmonization core, codifying five named substitution rules. Chapters 8-9 are appendices: the chord dictionary that backs the painting-by-numbers lookup, and a fingerstyle-technique appendix justifying the right-hand method underwriting the whole enterprise.

## The Four-Step Method as Central Thesis

The entire book is an elaboration of Chapter 1's four-step recipe, and almost every later rule is best read as a constraint, lookup table, or escape hatch attached to one of those four steps. Step 1 (tune selection) is constrained by the ballad-not-bebop rule: slower tempos with longer-held melody notes are practically easier for beginners. Step 2 (transposition) is constrained by the range rule of thumb - find a key where the melody sits on the top 3 strings between roughly the 3rd and 9th frets - explicitly defended against two failure modes (too high sounds thin, too low sounds dark and muddy). Step 3 (melody on the top strings) imposes a horizontal rather than positional fretting approach and is reaffirmed in Chapter 3 with an explicit carve-out that occasional dips to the 3rd or 4th string are acceptable. Step 4 (voicing the chords below the melody) is governed by the headline pedagogical rule that you don't need a chord on every melody note: chords go on long-held notes and at chord changes, exploiting the guitar's quick decay so that a chord change implies sustain rather than holding the note out. The four-step recipe is therefore not a beginner's onramp that the book later abandons - it is the load-bearing thesis the entire 250-plus pages of vocabulary, analysis, dictionary, and substitution machinery serves.

## The Painting-by-Numbers Lookup System

Chapters 3-4 supply the operational core that makes Step 4 of the four-step method mechanical rather than improvised. The linchpin construct is interval analysis: knowing each melody note's interval relative to its underlying chord is what enables dictionary lookup of a voicing with that interval on top. O'Rourke flags the most common beginner mistake - labeling intervals against the overall key rather than the current chord - and elevates this to his explicit 'golden rule of interval analysis,' backed by the major-scale-of-the-current-chord's-root reference rule. The closed chord-tone vocabulary used as dictionary index keys is fixed: 1, b9, 9, b3, 3, 11, #11, 5, b13, 13, b7, 7, plus PT (passing tone) as the escape hatch for unharmonized chromatic notes and an edge-case label for melody notes that anticipate the next chord. Dictionary lookup itself is keyed on three inputs - the melody string, the chord type at that moment, and the melody note's interval relative to that chord - and a dictionary voicing is transposed into the tune's key by sliding the shape until its top note lands on the melody fret. The painting-by-numbers rule itself is stated as: pick voicings whose top note is the same interval as the melody note relative to the underlying chord. The whole system is what makes the four-step method teachable rather than merely demonstrable.

## The Three Harmonic Families and the Chord Collection

Chapter 2's organizing taxonomy is that every chord belongs to one of three harmonic families - Major, Minor, or Dominant - with minor refined into m7 and m7b5 sub-families and dominant refined into natural ('7') and altered ('7alt') sub-families, where the sub-family distinction drives major-vs-minor ii-V-i voicing choice. The central indexing unit is the chord collection, defined as a group of chords covering every diatonic interval as the top note, which is what makes the dictionary indexable by melody note. The dictionary is structured around ii-V-I precisely because that single progression covers all three harmonic families, and collections are duplicated into 1st-string and 2nd-string variants to match the top-2-string melody constraint from Module 1. On the theory side, the chapter defines a chord symbol as 'an attempt to describe a sound,' establishes stacking thirds as the core construction principle, and explains 9/11/13 extensions as continuing to stack thirds past the 7th. The load-bearing pedagogical rule of Chapter 2 is the gap between the theoretically correct chord and the playable guitar voicing, filled by an explicit omittable-notes list: 5th, root, 11th, and 9th first, with 3rd and 7th sometimes omittable in complex chords. A final terminological rule - surrounding chords define a chord's name as much as its notes do - foreshadows the substitution machinery of Chapter 7.

## Restraint as Aesthetic Axiom

A single aesthetic commitment surfaces in every chapter and is the book's most consistent prescriptive voice: less is more. Chapter 1 states it as 'don't harmonize every note.' Chapter 4 restates it as 'less is more' (chords placed selectively are easier on the hands and usually imply the harmony adequately) and as 'playability over ambition' (easier voicings flow musically better). Chapter 4's three remediation strategies for awkward passages all express the same axiom: cut notes out of the chord, substitute a different harmonization device such as parallel octaves or diads, or remove the difficult voicing entirely - which O'Rourke explicitly identifies as his own most-used escape valve. Chapter 6 sanctions rhythmic adjustment of the melody to facilitate fretboard jumps, framing the arranger's pragmatics as a rule rather than a compromise. Chapter 7's substitution machinery closes with the explicit restraint rule that subtlety beats density: keep things simple and use substitutions occasionally for color. Color itself is localized: Chapter 4 locates the 'jazzy' sound specifically in extensions and tensions added to dominant chords as the targeted chord type for color, which is consistent with Chapter 8's dictionary structure (a dedicated section for altered/extended dominants as the method's color resource).

## The Solo-Guitar Extension and Its Devices

Chapter 5 extends the four-step method to solo guitar by adding bass responsibility to Step 4. The aesthetic strategy is alternation rather than simultaneity - walking bass, then chord line, then unaccompanied single line - because the listener's mind fills in the gaps to produce 'the illusion of solo guitar.' The load-bearing difference from trio playing is greater prominence of bass notes on the 5th and 6th strings since there is no bass player, and a specific key-selection rule follows: prefer A, E, or D so open strings can carry bass notes and free the fretting hand. The diagnostic for thin-sounding arrangements is bass voiced too close to the upper harmony. The solo-guitar voicing primitive is the shell voicing - root, 3rd, and 7th only - derived from bar chords by dropping the 5th first as the rule for which note to cut, with the 6th available as a substitute for the 7th for a warmer, more resolved color, and a general refinement rule to avoid doubled notes unless the doubling happens to sound good. The four core solo-guitar devices are defined explicitly: arpeggiation (plucking chord notes one at a time, used as a melody-space filler in slow ballads), octaves (Wes-Montgomery-style melody doubling one octave down, supported by the muting technique of resting the first finger gently on the unwanted in-between string), diads (Barney-Kessel-style parallel 3rds or 6ths as a primary melody-support layer), and walking bass (a four-step build filling beats 2 and 3 with diatonic, chromatic, or arpeggio notes between the beat-1 root and the beat-4 approach note). A chromatic approach-chord rule completes the toolkit: repeat the same voicing one fret away from the target. An intermediate drill - adding a bass note under the melody at each chord change - builds the ear before voicings get added.

## The Five Substitution Rules

Chapter 7 is the substitution-and-reharmonization core, opening with the foundational definition that a substitution simply means replacing one chord with another and then codifying five distinct named substitution rules. (1) The Tritone Substitution Rule: any chord can be replaced by one whose root is a tritone away, justified theoretically by the shared 3rd/7th guide tones that swap places between the two chords (the common-tones-enable-substitution principle that grounds the rest of the chapter). (2) The I-to-vi substitution rule and (3) the I-to-iii substitution rule, both grounded in relative-key thinking and the umbrella concept of chord addition. (4) The Minor for Dominant Substitution Rule, borrowed from Pat Martino's minor conversion concept and operationalized as substituting the iim7 from the dominant's 5th. (5) The back-cycling substitution rule, framed as movement through the circle of fourths - the most common root movement in Western music - and the melodic common-tone substitution rule that drives modern Rosenwinkel/Kreisberg-style colors. The chapter's characteristic pedagogical move pairs these rules with two concrete practice prescriptions: the metronome on beats 2 and 4 as the jazz-preferred form, and the load-bearing per-session quantum of just one or two bars worked slowly.

## The Fingerstyle Foundation

Chapter 9 (Appendix 2) is not peripheral - it justifies the right-hand method underwriting the entire enterprise with the claim that fingerstyle is what enables multiple simultaneous voices and therefore the whole chord-melody enterprise. It establishes the PIMAC finger-naming convention (p/i/m/a/c) used throughout the picking-pattern markings and devotes early space to ergonomic posture rules. The two canonical strokes are defined as the foundational pluck vocabulary - rest stroke (finger lands on the next string) and free stroke (finger flies free) - with the explicit preference rule that free stroke is the main technique for jazz guitar. O'Rourke's characteristic moves are tight mechanical specifications: thumb motion comes from the lowest joint with a small rowing-paddle circular motion reserved for bass notes and basslines; multi-string plucking requires the wrist rolled toward the headstock to keep thumb and index from obstructing each other. Load-bearing combinatorial rules close the chapter: the canonical two-finger pairs are p+i or i+m, the canonical three-finger groupings are pim or ima, voicings cap at four notes and the pinky is omitted from the picking hand, and plucked notes must sound simultaneously to register as a chord rather than an arpeggiation. Chapter 1 had already foreshadowed this with its plectrum-bass-then-strum technique for players using a pick - the right-hand technique choice is presented as one of the method's load-bearing inputs from the start.

## The Method as a Unit

Taken together, the book teaches an integrated method with five interlocking commitments. First, the four-step recipe (pick tune, transpose for range, melody on top strings, voicings below) is the book's central thesis and every other chapter is a constraint, lookup table, or escape hatch on one of those four steps. Second, the Three Harmonic Families taxonomy plus the chord collection construct makes the chord vocabulary indexable by melody note, which is what makes Step 4 mechanical rather than improvised. Third, the painting-by-numbers rule - pick voicings whose top note is the same interval as the melody note relative to the underlying chord - executes the lookup, governed by the golden-rule-of-interval-analysis (interval relates to the current chord, not the key) and a fixed closed vocabulary of chord-tone labels. Fourth, restraint is the aesthetic axiom that runs through every chapter: don't harmonize every note, less is more, playability over ambition, removing a voicing entirely is a sanctioned move, and substitutions are for occasional color rather than density. Fifth, solo-guitar arranging extends the trio method by adding bass responsibility on the 5th/6th strings, shell voicings as the primitive, and four named devices (arpeggiation, octaves, diads, walking bass), while Chapter 7's five named substitution rules supply the reharmonization toolkit and Chapter 9's fingerstyle technique supplies the right-hand foundation that makes simultaneous voices physically possible. The book's pedagogical voice throughout is procedural and permissive: rules are stated, then explicit carve-outs and escape valves are sanctioned (cut notes, substitute diads/octaves, remove the chord, rewrite the melody's rhythm), so that the four-step recipe remains executable even when its strict output isn't playable.
